skills.skill: Kinematics (Motion in One and Two Dimensions)
skills.teks_description: Describe one-dimensional motion using distance, displacement, speed, velocity, frames of reference, and acceleration
skills.prompt: This topic covers kinematics for Physics students (TEKS Phy.5(C)). Students should describe and analyze motion in one and two dimensions using mathematical relationships. One-dimensional kinematics equations: v = v0 + at, x = x0 + v0t + ½at², v² = v0² + 2a(x - x0), x = ½(v + v0)t. Key concepts: distinguish distance (scalar) from displacement (vector), speed (scalar) from velocity (vector), average vs. instantaneous values. Motion graphs: position-time (slope = velocity), velocity-time (slope = acceleration, area = displacement), acceleration-time graphs. Two-dimensional motion: projectile motion combines constant horizontal velocity with vertical acceleration due to gravity (g = 9.8 m/s²); horizontal and vertical components are independent. Free fall: all objects accelerate at g regardless of mass (ignoring air resistance). Frames of reference: motion is relative to the observer. Vocabulary: displacement, velocity, acceleration, scalar, vector, projectile, trajectory, free fall, frame of reference, component. Common misconceptions: heavier objects fall faster; an object at the highest point of its trajectory has zero acceleration.

skills.skill: Forces and Newton's Laws of Motion
skills.teks_description: Calculate force effects using free body diagrams and Newton's second law
skills.prompt: This topic covers forces and Newton's laws for Physics students (TEKS Phy.5(F)). Students should analyze forces using free body diagrams and apply Newton's laws. Newton's First Law (Inertia): an object at rest stays at rest and an object in motion stays in motion unless acted upon by a net force. Newton's Second Law: F_net = ma; acceleration is directly proportional to net force and inversely proportional to mass. Newton's Third Law: for every action force, there is an equal and opposite reaction force. Free body diagrams: show all forces acting on an object (gravity, normal force, tension, friction, applied force). Friction: static friction (prevents motion, fs ≤ μsN) and kinetic friction (opposes motion, fk = μkN). Weight vs. mass: weight = mg (force, measured in Newtons), mass is amount of matter (kg). Key concepts: net force, equilibrium (Fnet = 0), inclined planes (resolve forces into parallel and perpendicular components), connected objects, Atwood machines. Vocabulary: inertia, net force, normal force, tension, friction coefficient, equilibrium, free body diagram, newton, acceleration. Common misconceptions: heavier objects need more force to move at constant speed on frictionless surfaces; force is needed to maintain motion.

skills.teks_description: Calculate and explain work and power; identify when work occurs
skills.prompt: This topic covers work, energy, and power for Physics students (TEKS Phy.7(A)). Students should calculate work and power and understand energy transformations. Work: W = Fd cos(θ), where F is force, d is displacement, and θ is the angle between force and displacement direction; measured in joules (J). Work is done only when force causes displacement. Power: P = W/t = Fv, the rate of doing work; measured in watts (W). Types of energy: kinetic energy KE = ½mv², gravitational potential energy PE = mgh, elastic potential energy PE = ½kx². Conservation of energy: total mechanical energy (KE + PE) is conserved in the absence of non-conservative forces. Work-energy theorem: net work equals change in kinetic energy (Wnet = ΔKE). Key concepts: conservative forces (gravity, springs) vs. non-conservative forces (friction, air resistance), energy transformations in real systems (some energy converts to thermal energy via friction). Vocabulary: work, energy, power, kinetic energy, potential energy, mechanical energy, conservation of energy, watt, joule, conservative force. Common misconceptions: holding a heavy object requires work; energy is used up rather than transformed.

skills.teks_description: Calculate impulse and momentum in physical systems
skills.prompt: This topic covers momentum and impulse for Physics students (TEKS Phy.7(D)). Students should calculate and apply momentum and impulse in physical systems. Momentum: p = mv (mass × velocity), a vector quantity measured in kg·m/s. Impulse: J = FΔt = Δp (force × time interval = change in momentum). Impulse-momentum theorem: the impulse on an object equals its change in momentum. Conservation of momentum: in an isolated system (no external forces), total momentum before = total momentum after. Types of collisions: elastic (both momentum and kinetic energy conserved, objects bounce apart), inelastic (momentum conserved but kinetic energy not, objects may deform), perfectly inelastic (objects stick together, maximum KE loss). Key equations: m1v1i + m2v2i = m1v1f + m2v2f (general), m1v1i + m2v2i = (m1 + m2)vf (perfectly inelastic). Applications: car safety (airbags increase time to reduce force), sports (follow-through increases impulse), rocket propulsion. Vocabulary: momentum, impulse, conservation of momentum, elastic collision, inelastic collision, isolated system, recoil. Common misconceptions: heavier objects always have more momentum; momentum is the same as force.

skills.teks_description: Describe and analyze uniform circular and projectile motion in two dimensions
skills.prompt: This topic covers circular motion and rotation for Physics students (TEKS Phy.5(D)). Students should analyze uniform circular motion and projectile motion in two dimensions. Uniform circular motion: object moves in a circle at constant speed but changing velocity (direction changes). Centripetal acceleration: ac = v²/r, always directed toward the center of the circle. Centripetal force: Fc = mv²/r, the net force causing circular motion (not a separate force but provided by tension, gravity, friction, or normal force). Period: T = 2πr/v, time for one complete revolution. Frequency: f = 1/T, revolutions per unit time. Projectile motion: combination of horizontal (constant velocity) and vertical (constant acceleration g = 9.8 m/s²) motion; trajectory is parabolic. Range, maximum height, and time of flight depend on launch angle and initial velocity. Key concepts: centripetal vs. centrifugal (centrifugal is a fictitious force in rotating reference frames), banking of curves, satellites in orbit. Vocabulary: centripetal, centrifugal, period, frequency, angular velocity, tangential velocity, radians, projectile, trajectory, range. Common misconceptions: centrifugal force pushes objects outward; objects in circular motion are in equilibrium.

skills.teks_description: Calculate gravitational force magnitude using Newton's law of universal gravitation
skills.prompt: This topic covers gravitation for Physics students (TEKS Phy.5(H)). Students should understand and apply Newton's law of universal gravitation. Newton's law of universal gravitation: F = Gm1m2/r², where G = 6.674 × 10^-11 N·m²/kg² is the gravitational constant, m1 and m2 are the masses of two objects, and r is the distance between their centers. Key concepts: gravitational force is always attractive, acts between all masses, and follows an inverse-square relationship with distance. Gravitational field strength: g = GM/r², on Earth's surface g ≈ 9.8 m/s². Orbital motion: satellites maintain orbit when gravitational force provides centripetal force (Gm1m2/r² = mv²/r). Kepler's laws: (1) planets orbit in ellipses with the sun at one focus, (2) equal areas in equal times, (3) T² ∝ r³. Weight varies by location (depends on local g), mass is constant. Escape velocity: minimum speed to escape gravitational field. Vocabulary: gravitational force, gravitational constant, gravitational field, orbit, satellite, weight, mass, inverse-square law, Kepler's laws, escape velocity. Common misconceptions: gravity only exists on Earth; astronauts are weightless because there is no gravity in space (they are in free fall).

skills.teks_description: Examine simple harmonic motion and wave energy propagation in various media
skills.prompt: This topic covers simple harmonic motion (SHM) for Physics students (TEKS Phy.8(A)). Students should understand oscillatory motion and wave energy propagation. SHM occurs when a restoring force is proportional to displacement from equilibrium (F = -kx for springs). Examples: mass on a spring, simple pendulum (for small angles). Key equations: period of spring T = 2π√(m/k), period of pendulum T = 2π√(L/g). Energy in SHM: continuous exchange between kinetic and potential energy; total mechanical energy is conserved. At equilibrium: maximum KE, zero PE; at maximum displacement (amplitude): zero KE, maximum PE. Position as function of time: x(t) = A cos(ωt + φ), where A is amplitude, ω = 2π/T is angular frequency, φ is phase constant. Key concepts: frequency, period, amplitude, equilibrium position, restoring force, damping (amplitude decreases over time due to friction), resonance (driving frequency matches natural frequency, maximum amplitude). Vocabulary: oscillation, simple harmonic motion, amplitude, period, frequency, restoring force, equilibrium, damping, resonance, angular frequency, phase. Common misconceptions: period depends on amplitude; heavier pendulums swing faster.

skills.teks_description: Investigate wave characteristics: velocity, frequency, amplitude, wavelength relationships
skills.prompt: This topic covers waves and sound for Physics students (TEKS Phy.8(C)). Students should investigate wave characteristics and mathematical relationships. Wave types: transverse (displacement perpendicular to propagation, e.g., light, water surface) and longitudinal (displacement parallel to propagation, e.g., sound, compression waves). Wave equation: v = fλ, where v is velocity, f is frequency, and λ is wavelength. Key properties: amplitude (maximum displacement, relates to energy/loudness), frequency (cycles per second, Hz, relates to pitch), wavelength (distance between consecutive crests), period T = 1/f. Sound waves: longitudinal mechanical waves requiring a medium; speed depends on medium (faster in solids than gases); speed in air ≈ 343 m/s at 20°C. Sound properties: pitch (frequency), loudness (amplitude), quality/timbre (waveform). Doppler effect: perceived frequency changes when source or observer moves; approaching = higher pitch, receding = lower pitch. Vocabulary: crest, trough, compression, rarefaction, wavelength, frequency, amplitude, period, medium, Doppler effect, transverse, longitudinal. Common misconceptions: sound travels in vacuum; louder sounds travel faster; the medium moves with the wave.

skills.teks_description: Identify electric and magnetic forces in everyday applications including generators, motors, and transformers
skills.prompt: This topic covers electromagnetism for Physics students (TEKS Phy.6(B)). Students should understand the relationship between electricity and magnetism in everyday applications. Key concepts: electric currents produce magnetic fields (Oersted's discovery), moving magnetic fields produce electric currents (Faraday's discovery). Electromagnets: coils of wire carrying current create controllable magnetic fields; strength depends on current, number of coils, and core material. Applications: electric motors (convert electrical energy to mechanical energy using magnetic force on current-carrying conductors in a magnetic field), generators (convert mechanical energy to electrical energy via electromagnetic induction), transformers (change voltage levels using two coils with a shared magnetic core; Vs/Vp = Ns/Np). Key principles: right-hand rule for direction of magnetic field around a wire, Lenz's law (induced current opposes the change causing it). Vocabulary: electromagnet, electromagnetic induction, Faraday's law, Lenz's law, motor, generator, transformer, solenoid, magnetic flux, eddy current, alternating current, direct current. Common misconceptions: magnets and electricity are unrelated; transformers create energy; permanent magnets never lose their magnetism.

skills.teks_description: Predict electric force magnitude between objects using Coulomb's law
skills.prompt: This topic covers electric charge and electric fields for Physics students (TEKS Phy.6(A)). Students should understand electric forces and predict their behavior using Coulomb's law. Electric charge: two types (positive and negative), like charges repel, opposite charges attract. Charge is quantized (multiples of e = 1.6 × 10^-19 C) and conserved. Charging methods: friction (triboelectric), conduction (direct contact), induction (rearrangement without contact). Coulomb's law: F = kq1q2/r², where k = 8.99 × 10^9 N·m²/C²; force is proportional to product of charges and inversely proportional to square of distance. Electric field: E = F/q = kQ/r², region around a charge where other charges experience a force; field lines point away from positive charges and toward negative charges. Key concepts: superposition principle (net field is vector sum of individual fields), conductors vs. insulators, grounding, electrostatic shielding. Vocabulary: charge, coulomb, electric field, field lines, Coulomb's law, conductor, insulator, grounding, induction, electroscope, polarization. Common misconceptions: electrons are the only charge carriers; electric field lines can cross; neutral objects have no charges.

skills.teks_description: Calculate current, potential difference, resistance, and power using Ohm's law
skills.prompt: This topic covers electric potential and capacitance for Physics students (TEKS Phy.6(E)). Students should understand voltage, current, resistance, and power relationships. Electric potential (voltage): V = W/q, the electric potential energy per unit charge, measured in volts (V = J/C). Potential difference drives current flow. Current: I = q/t, the rate of charge flow, measured in amperes (A). Resistance: R = V/I (Ohm's law), opposition to current flow, measured in ohms (Ω). Factors affecting resistance: material (resistivity), length (longer = more resistance), cross-sectional area (larger = less resistance), temperature. Power: P = IV = I²R = V²/R, rate of energy conversion, measured in watts (W). Electrical energy: E = Pt, measured in joules or kilowatt-hours (kWh). Capacitance: C = q/V, ability to store charge, measured in farads (F). Energy stored in capacitor: U = ½CV². Key concepts: voltage sources, electrical safety, energy efficiency, household circuits. Vocabulary: voltage, current, resistance, Ohm's law, power, capacitance, capacitor, resistivity, conductivity, potential difference. Common misconceptions: current is used up in a circuit; voltage and current are the same thing; batteries store electrons.

skills.teks_description: Analyze and construct series and parallel circuits using schematics
skills.prompt: This topic covers electric circuits for Physics students (TEKS Phy.6(D)). Students should analyze and construct series and parallel circuits. Series circuits: components connected end-to-end in a single path; current is the same through all components (I_total = I1 = I2); voltages add (V_total = V1 + V2); resistances add (R_total = R1 + R2); if one component fails, circuit breaks. Parallel circuits: components connected across the same two points; voltage is the same across all branches (V_total = V1 = V2); currents add (I_total = I1 + I2); reciprocal resistances add (1/R_total = 1/R1 + 1/R2); if one branch fails, others continue. Circuit components: battery/voltage source, resistor, switch, ammeter (series), voltmeter (parallel). Kirchhoff's laws: junction rule (currents into junction = currents out), loop rule (sum of voltage changes around closed loop = 0). Key concepts: reading circuit diagrams, using multimeters, calculating equivalent resistance for combination circuits. Vocabulary: series, parallel, equivalent resistance, junction, loop, ammeter, voltmeter, short circuit, open circuit, schematic, Kirchhoff's laws. Common misconceptions: current is used up by resistors; a short circuit has high resistance.

skills.teks_description: Identify electric and magnetic forces and fields in everyday applications
skills.prompt: This topic covers magnetic fields and forces for Physics students (TEKS Phy.6(B)). Students should understand magnetic fields, their sources, and their interactions with moving charges and currents. Magnetic fields: produced by moving charges, current-carrying wires, and permanent magnets. Field lines: exit north pole, enter south pole, never cross. Earth has a magnetic field (geographic north is near magnetic south). Force on moving charge: F = qvB sin(θ), force is perpendicular to both velocity and field (use right-hand rule for positive charges). Force on current-carrying wire: F = BIL sin(θ), where B is field strength, I is current, L is wire length. Magnetic field from straight wire: B = μ0I/(2πr). Solenoid: coil of wire creates uniform interior field, B = μ0nI. Key concepts: right-hand rules (for force direction and field direction), magnetic domains in permanent magnets, ferromagnetic materials (iron, nickel, cobalt), diamagnetic and paramagnetic materials. Vocabulary: magnetic field, magnetic force, tesla, right-hand rule, solenoid, magnetic domain, ferromagnetic, north pole, south pole, magnetic flux. Common misconceptions: magnetic poles can be isolated (no magnetic monopoles); all metals are magnetic.

skills.teks_description: Identify electromagnetic induction applications including generators, motors, and transformers
skills.prompt: This topic covers electromagnetic induction for Physics students (TEKS Phy.6(B)). Students should understand how changing magnetic fields produce electric currents and their applications. Faraday's law: a changing magnetic flux through a loop induces an electromotive force (emf); emf = -NΔΦ/Δt, where N is number of loops and Φ = BA cos(θ) is magnetic flux. Lenz's law: the induced current flows in a direction that opposes the change in flux that caused it (conservation of energy). Methods to change flux: change B (field strength), change A (area of loop), change θ (angle between field and loop normal), move loop relative to field. Applications: generators (rotate coil in magnetic field to produce AC), transformers (change voltage using two coils; Vs/Vp = Ns/Np; step-up increases voltage, step-down decreases it), eddy currents (induced currents in bulk conductors, used in braking systems and metal detectors). Key concepts: AC vs. DC, mutual inductance, self-inductance, back-emf in motors. Vocabulary: electromagnetic induction, magnetic flux, Faraday's law, Lenz's law, emf, generator, transformer, eddy current, inductance, alternating current. Common misconceptions: a stationary magnet near a coil produces current; transformers work with DC.

skills.teks_description: Describe image formation through plane mirror reflection and convex lens refraction
skills.prompt: This topic covers light and optics for Physics students (TEKS Phy.8(G)). Students should understand image formation through reflection and refraction. Light: electromagnetic wave, travels at c = 3 × 10^8 m/s in vacuum, exhibits both wave and particle properties. Reflection: law of reflection (angle of incidence = angle of reflection). Plane mirrors: produce virtual, upright, same-size images. Curved mirrors: concave (converging) and convex (diverging); mirror equation 1/f = 1/do + 1/di; magnification M = -di/do. Refraction: bending of light when passing between media with different optical densities; Snell's law: n1 sin(θ1) = n2 sin(θ2); index of refraction n = c/v. Total internal reflection occurs when light travels from denser to less dense medium at angles greater than the critical angle. Lenses: convex (converging) and concave (diverging); thin lens equation 1/f = 1/do + 1/di. Key concepts: real vs. virtual images, focal point, ray diagrams for mirrors and lenses. Vocabulary: reflection, refraction, index of refraction, focal length, virtual image, real image, magnification, critical angle, total internal reflection, dispersion. Common misconceptions: mirrors reverse left and right; light always travels in straight lines.

skills.teks_description: Investigate wave behaviors: reflection, refraction, diffraction, interference, Doppler effect, polarization
skills.prompt: This topic covers reflection and refraction wave behaviors for Physics students (TEKS Phy.8(D)). Students should investigate multiple wave behaviors including reflection, refraction, diffraction, interference, the Doppler effect, and polarization. Reflection: waves bounce off boundaries; angle of incidence equals angle of reflection; fixed-end reflection inverts the wave, free-end reflection does not. Refraction: waves change direction when entering a new medium due to speed change. Diffraction: waves bend around obstacles or spread through openings; more pronounced when obstacle/opening size is comparable to wavelength. Interference: superposition of waves; constructive (waves in phase, amplitudes add) and destructive (waves out of phase, amplitudes cancel). Standing waves: result of interference between incident and reflected waves, creating nodes (no displacement) and antinodes (maximum displacement). Doppler effect: frequency shift due to relative motion between source and observer. Polarization: restricting wave vibration to one plane (only transverse waves can be polarized). Vocabulary: reflection, refraction, diffraction, interference, constructive, destructive, standing wave, node, antinode, Doppler effect, polarization, superposition. Common misconceptions: diffraction only occurs with light; destructive interference destroys energy.

skills.teks_description: Apply conservation of energy using work-energy theorem and energy transformations
skills.prompt: This topic covers thermodynamics for Physics students (TEKS Phy.7(C)). Students should apply conservation of energy principles including thermal energy transformations. Laws of thermodynamics: Zeroth law (thermal equilibrium is transitive), First law (ΔU = Q - W, conservation of energy; internal energy change equals heat added minus work done), Second law (entropy of an isolated system never decreases; heat flows from hot to cold spontaneously), Third law (absolute zero cannot be reached). Key concepts: internal energy (sum of kinetic and potential energies of all molecules), temperature (measure of average kinetic energy), heat (energy transfer due to temperature difference). Work done by gas: W = PΔV (at constant pressure). Thermodynamic processes: isothermal (constant T), isobaric (constant P), isochoric/isometric (constant V), adiabatic (no heat transfer). Heat engines: convert thermal energy to work; efficiency = W/Qh = 1 - Qc/Qh; Carnot efficiency = 1 - Tc/Th. Vocabulary: internal energy, entropy, enthalpy, heat engine, refrigerator, isothermal, adiabatic, isobaric, isochoric, Carnot cycle, thermal equilibrium. Common misconceptions: cold is transferred to objects; heat and temperature are identical.

skills.teks_description: Apply conservation of energy including thermal energy transformations and transfer
skills.prompt: This topic covers heat transfer for Physics students (TEKS Phy.7(C)). Students should understand how thermal energy moves through systems. Three modes of heat transfer: conduction (direct contact, molecular collisions, dominant in solids; rate depends on conductivity, area, temperature difference, and thickness: Q/t = kAΔT/L), convection (bulk fluid motion, natural convection driven by density differences from temperature, forced convection by fans/pumps; occurs in fluids), radiation (electromagnetic waves, no medium required, all objects emit radiation; Stefan-Boltzmann law: P = εσAT⁴). Specific heat: Q = mcΔT, where c is specific heat capacity; water has high specific heat (4,184 J/kg·°C). Latent heat: Q = mL, energy for phase changes without temperature change; heat of fusion (Lf) for melting/freezing, heat of vaporization (Lv) for boiling/condensing. Thermal conductors (metals, high k values) vs. insulators (wood, plastic, air, low k values). Key concepts: thermal equilibrium, calorimetry, R-value for insulation, greenhouse effect. Vocabulary: conduction, convection, radiation, specific heat, latent heat, thermal conductivity, insulator, conductor, calorimetry, thermal equilibrium, emissivity. Common misconceptions: metals are cold (they conduct heat away from hands); radiation requires a medium.

skills.teks_description: Describe photoelectric effect and emission spectra explained by photon model
skills.prompt: This topic covers quantum physics basics for Physics students (TEKS Phy.9(A)). Students should understand the photoelectric effect and emission spectra as evidence for the photon model. Photoelectric effect: light shining on metal surface ejects electrons; key observations that classical wave theory could not explain: (1) electrons ejected only above threshold frequency regardless of intensity, (2) kinetic energy of ejected electrons depends on frequency not intensity, (3) emission is instantaneous. Einstein's explanation: light consists of photons with energy E = hf (h = 6.626 × 10^-34 J·s is Planck's constant, f is frequency); work function φ is minimum energy to eject electron; KE_max = hf - φ. Emission spectra: atoms emit light at specific discrete frequencies when excited electrons transition to lower energy levels; each element has unique spectrum (atomic fingerprint). Bohr model: electrons occupy discrete energy levels; energy is emitted or absorbed when electrons transition between levels, E = hf. Key concepts: wave-particle duality, quantization of energy, de Broglie wavelength λ = h/mv. Vocabulary: photon, photoelectric effect, work function, threshold frequency, emission spectrum, absorption spectrum, Planck's constant, quantized, wave-particle duality. Common misconceptions: light is purely a wave; brighter light always ejects electrons.

skills.teks_description: Provide quantum phenomena examples including Heisenberg uncertainty principle and quantum computing
skills.prompt: This topic covers nuclear physics basics for Physics students (TEKS Phy.9(D)). Students should understand quantum phenomena and nuclear applications. Quantum principles: Heisenberg uncertainty principle (cannot simultaneously know exact position and momentum: ΔxΔp ≥ ℏ/2), wave-particle duality (all matter exhibits wave properties, significant only at atomic scale), quantum tunneling (particles can pass through energy barriers they classically could not overcome). Nuclear structure: nucleus contains protons and neutrons (nucleons), held together by strong nuclear force that overcomes electromagnetic repulsion at short ranges. Nuclear reactions: fission (heavy nuclei split, used in nuclear power and weapons), fusion (light nuclei combine, powers stars, potential future energy source). Mass-energy equivalence: E = mc², small mass defect in nuclei corresponds to large binding energy. Radioactivity: alpha, beta, gamma decay; half-life describes decay rate. Applications: nuclear power, medical imaging (PET scans), radiation therapy, carbon dating, quantum computing. Vocabulary: uncertainty principle, wave-particle duality, quantum tunneling, strong nuclear force, binding energy, mass defect, fission, fusion, half-life, quantum computing. Common misconceptions: quantum effects are visible at macroscopic scales; nuclear power always produces nuclear bombs.
